section.title: Appointing Elders on Crete
section.sectionRationale: This is the reason Paul left Titus on Crete: to finish what was unfinished by appointing qualified elders in every town. Paul sets out the character of these leaders, then turns to the false teachers who must be silenced, quoting even a Cretan prophet against them. Leadership and the guarding of truth belong together in one movement.
section.thesis: Healthy churches need godly, blameless, doctrinally sound leaders who can both hold out the faithful word and stop the mouths of deceivers whose empty religion is exposed by their unfit lives.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the charge to appoint elders and the character required of an overseer; then the confrontation of the rebellious false teachers on Crete, whose profession is denied by their works.

verses.text: The reason I left you in Crete was that you would set in order what was unfinished and appoint {{presbyteros|elders}} in every town, as I directed you.
An elder must be {{anenkletos|blameless}}, the husband of but one wife, having children who are believers and who are not open to accusation of indiscretion or insubordination.
As God’s {{oikonomos|steward}}, an {{episkopos|overseer}} must be above reproach, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to drunkenness, not violent, not greedy for money.
Instead, he must be hospitable, a lover of good, {{sophron|self-controlled}}, upright, holy, and disciplined.
He must hold firmly to the faithful word as it was taught, so that he can encourage others by {{hygiaino|sound}} teaching and refute those who contradict it.

units.label: Silence the false teachers
units.note: empty talk denied by their works

verses.text: For many are rebellious and full of empty talk and deception, especially those of the circumcision,
who must be silenced. For the sake of dishonorable gain, they undermine entire households and teach things they should not.
As one of their own prophets has said, “Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.”
This testimony is true. Therefore rebuke them sternly, so that they will be {{hygiaino|sound}} in the faith
and will pay no attention to Jewish myths or to the commands of men who have rejected the truth.
To the pure, all things are pure; but to the defiled and unbelieving, nothing is pure. Indeed, both their minds and their consciences are defiled.
They profess to know God, but by their actions they deny Him. They are detestable, disobedient, and unfit for any good deed.

presbyteros.gloss: An elder, a mature, appointed leader of the church. The term stresses spiritual maturity and standing. In verse 7 Paul uses overseer for the same office, showing that elder and overseer describe one role from two angles: seniority and responsibility. Every town was to have such tested men.

anenkletos.gloss: Blameless, above reproach, with no charge that can be made to stick. It does not mean sinless but of a settled, unimpeachable reputation. It heads the list of elder qualifications and is repeated in verse 7, framing the whole character portrait: a leader whose life gives critics nothing to grab.

oikonomos.gloss: A household manager or steward, entrusted with another's affairs and answerable to the master. The overseer manages God's household, the church, not as owner but as trustee. This frames leadership as stewardship: faithful care of what belongs to God, to be given back with an account.

episkopos.gloss: An overseer or guardian, one who watches over and cares for. Paul moves seamlessly from elder in verse 5 to overseer in verse 7, treating them as the same office. The word underlines the shepherd's watchful, protective duty toward the flock and the truth.

sophron.gloss: Self-controlled, sensible, of a sound and disciplined mind. It is a signature virtue of Titus, urged on elders, older men, young women, and young men alike. Against Crete's reputation for excess, this sober self-mastery is to mark God's people from top to bottom.

hygiaino.gloss: To be healthy or sound, the root of our word hygiene. Paul applies it to doctrine and to faith: teaching that is spiritually wholesome and health-giving, over against the diseased talk of the deceivers. Sound teaching in verse 9 makes for people who are sound in the faith in verse 13.

basic.context: Paul reminds Titus why he was left on Crete: to finish organising the churches by appointing elders in every town. He lists the kind of character these leaders must have, then describes the false teachers troubling the island, who must be silenced because their empty talk and greedy motives are wrecking households.
basic.hermeneutics: The list of qualifications is a pattern for choosing church leaders, focused far more on proven character than on talent or charisma. The sharp words about Cretans come from one of their own poets and are used to press the churches toward maturity, not to insult a whole people forever.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul uses two words for the one office: presbyteros, elder, and episkopos, overseer. The leader must be anenkletos, blameless, and an oikonomos, a steward of God's household. He must be sophron, self-controlled, a key Titus word, and able to give hygiaino, sound or healthy, teaching that keeps people sound in the faith.
basic.typology: The overseer as God's steward looks back to faithful stewards in Scripture and forward to Christ, the true and faithful Shepherd and Overseer of our souls. The godly elder is a small picture of the Chief Shepherd who cares perfectly for His flock.
basic.christ: Elders are undershepherds who point to Christ, the Chief Shepherd and Overseer of souls. They hold firmly to the faithful word, the gospel of Christ, and guard it against error. Their whole task exists to keep the church anchored to Jesus and His truth.
basic.god: God entrusts His household, the church, to stewards who must answer to Him. He cares that His people are led by those whose lives match their message, and He is not fooled by a profession of knowing Him that is contradicted by ungodly living.
basic.crossReferences: First Timothy 3 gives a parallel list of qualifications for overseers. First Peter 5 calls elders to shepherd God's flock under the Chief Shepherd. And Matthew 7 warns that a tree is known by its fruit, as these teachers are exposed by their works.
basic.oneStory: God has always gathered His people under shepherds, and He guards that flock by giving it faithful, godly leaders and by exposing false ones. Titus is to plant such leadership in Crete so the gospel takes deep root, part of God's larger work of building a people for Himself under the true Shepherd, Christ.

context.literary: The body of the letter opens with a purpose statement, verse 5, then a tightly structured qualification list, then its dark counterpart: the character of true elders set against the character of false teachers. The Cretan quotation in verse 12 is a striking rhetorical move that bridges the two.
context.historical: Crete's churches were young and scattered across many towns, still lacking settled leadership. Judaizing teachers, those of the circumcision, were spreading myths and commands of men for profit. Cretans had a proverbial reputation for dishonesty, so much so that to cretize meant to lie, which Paul turns to his argument.
context.canonical: With 1 Timothy 3, this is one of the two great New Testament passages on the qualifications for church office. It fixes the enduring principle that leadership in Christ's church is a matter first of tested character and sound doctrine.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To direct Titus in appointing qualified elders and to arm him to confront and silence the false teachers, so that the Cretan churches would be both well led and doctrinally protected.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The qualification list is prescriptive as an enduring pattern for choosing elders, though the specific vices and virtues are examples of the underlying character required. The Cretan quotation describes a cultural reputation Paul uses for a purpose; it is not a timeless verdict on an ethnic group.

debates.question: What does the husband of but one wife require?

views.label: A faithful, one-woman man
views.case: The phrase describes marital faithfulness and sexual integrity, a man devoted to his wife, as the BSB footnote suggests, rather than mandating marriage.

views.label: A restriction on remarriage or polygamy
views.case: Some read it to exclude polygamists or those improperly remarried, keeping the leader's household above reproach in a culture of loose marriage.

debates.bearing: Both readings land on the same target: an elder's marriage and home must be marked by fidelity and integrity that give no ground for accusation.

debates.question: How should the harsh Cretan quotation be understood?

views.label: A true cultural indictment used pastorally
views.case: Paul cites a Cretan prophet, Epimenides, to confirm that the local culture was steeped in vice, precisely so the churches would be rebuked into soundness, verse 13.

views.label: Not a blanket condemnation of individuals
views.case: It names a proverbial reputation, not a claim that every Cretan is beyond hope; the very letter aims at Cretans becoming sound in the faith.

debates.bearing: The quotation drives the pastoral goal: stern rebuke aimed at repentance and health, not contempt that writes people off.

study.originalLanguages: Paul names the one office with two words: presbyteros, elder, stressing mature standing, and episkopos, overseer, stressing watchful care. That overseer is God's oikonomos, a household steward who manages what belongs to the Master and must answer for it. The character required begins and ends with anenkletos, blameless, a reputation critics cannot dent, and includes sophron, self-controlled, the sober self-mastery that recurs all through Titus. The positive aim is hygiaino teaching, spiritually healthy and health-giving doctrine, set against the diseased empty talk of the deceivers; sound teaching in verse 9 is meant to make believers sound in the faith in verse 13.

typology.type: The overseer as God's steward of the household
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ the faithful Son over God's house, Hebrews 3:6, and to every leader who must give account to Him.

typology.type: Elders as shepherds of the flock
typology.fulfillment: Undershepherds of Christ the Chief Shepherd and Overseer of souls, 1 Peter 5:4 and 2:25.

study.christ: Elders are undershepherds who serve under Christ, the Chief Shepherd and Overseer of our souls, 1 Peter 2:25 and 5:4. Their central task is to hold firmly to the faithful word, the gospel of Christ handed down, and to guard it from those who would twist it. In their blameless character they are meant to reflect, however faintly, the perfect faithfulness of the Son who is over God's house; and their whole ministry exists to keep the church fastened to Jesus and His truth.
study.god: God is the Master of the household; the church is His, and its leaders are His stewards, answerable to Him. He cares deeply that those who lead His people live lives that match the message. He sees through hollow religion: those who profess to know Him but deny Him by their works are exposed, for to the pure all things are pure, but a defiled mind and conscience corrupt everything. God will not be mocked by a knowledge of Him that bears no fruit.

crossReferences.note: The parallel list of overseer qualifications, centred like this one on tested character.
Elders shepherd God's flock willingly, as examples, under the Chief Shepherd.
Overseers must guard the flock against savage wolves and distorters of the truth, as Titus must silence the deceivers.
You will know them by their fruits, the principle behind those who deny God by their actions.

study.oneStory: God has always shepherded His people through appointed leaders and has guarded them by raising up faithful ones and exposing false ones. Titus is to establish such leadership across Crete so the gospel is preserved and the churches grow strong. It is one scene in God's long work of gathering a people for Himself under the true Shepherd and Overseer of souls, Jesus Christ, who keeps His church in the truth.

sermon.title: Elders, Part One
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in Titus, this message expounds Titus 1:5-9, the charge to appoint elders and the character of the overseer, showing why the church needs leaders whose lives match the faithful word they hold. A careful, orthodox treatment of this movement's core.

quiz.q: Why did Paul leave Titus on Crete?

options.text: To set in order what was unfinished and appoint elders in every town.
options.note: This is Paul's stated reason in verse 5.

options.text: To collect money from the churches.
options.note: Paul in fact warns against leaders greedy for gain; his aim was godly order.

options.text: To start entirely new churches from scratch.
options.note: The churches existed already; Titus was to finish organising and lead them.

options.text: To take a rest from ministry.
options.note: Titus was left for demanding work, not rest.

quiz.q: What does Paul emphasise most in the qualifications for an elder?

options.text: Proven, blameless character and soundness in the faith.
options.note: The list is dominated by character and holding firmly to the word, verses 6-9.

options.text: Impressive speaking ability and popularity.
options.note: He must teach soundly, but the stress is on character, not charisma.

options.text: Wealth and social standing.
options.note: Paul warns against greed for money; status is not the point.

options.text: Formal education credentials.
options.note: The focus is godly character and grasp of the faithful word, not diplomas.

quiz.q: What are the two purposes of an elder holding firmly to the faithful word?

options.text: To encourage others by sound teaching and to refute those who contradict it.
options.note: Verse 9 names both the positive and the corrective task.

options.text: To win debates and build a personal following.
options.note: Refuting error is real, but the aim is the church's health, not a following.

options.text: To keep the teaching secret from outsiders.
options.note: The word is to be taught and defended openly, not hidden.

options.text: To replace Scripture with his own ideas.
options.note: He holds firmly to the word as taught, not his own inventions.

quiz.q: What was wrong with the false teachers on Crete?

options.text: They spread empty talk and deception for gain, wrecking households.
options.note: Verses 10-11 describe rebellious deceivers driven by dishonorable gain.

options.text: They simply used a different worship style.
options.note: The issue was false doctrine and corrupt motives, not style.

options.text: They were too strict about minor rules.
options.note: They pushed Jewish myths and human commands, but the root was rejecting the truth.

options.text: They preached the gospel too plainly.
options.note: They opposed the truth; their teaching was the problem.

quiz.q: How does Paul expose those who claim to know God but live ungodly lives?

options.text: They profess to know God, but by their actions they deny Him.
options.note: Verse 16 shows profession contradicted by conduct.

options.text: They know God fully and simply need more information.
options.note: Their problem is not lack of information but a defiled mind and denying works.

options.text: Their words matter and their actions do not.
options.note: Paul insists actions reveal the reality of a profession.

options.text: They are fully accepted despite their conduct.
options.note: Paul calls them unfit for any good deed; conduct exposes the claim.

questions: Paul chooses leaders by character before gifting. When you look for people to follow or to entrust with responsibility, how much weight do you give to proven character?
An elder is God's steward, managing what belongs to the Master. Where has God made you a steward of people, resources, or truth, and how faithfully are you handling it?
Self-control runs all through Titus. In which area of life is God calling you toward greater self-mastery right now?
The false teachers professed to know God but denied Him by their actions. Where might your daily conduct be sending a different message from your words about God?
Sound teaching is meant to keep us healthy in the faith. Who are the faithful voices you are letting shape you, and are you giving weight to teaching that is genuinely sound?

translationNotes.note: The husband of but one wife translates a phrase literally meaning a one-woman man; the BSB footnote offers faithful to his wife, stressing marital fidelity.
Where the BSB prints an em dash after above reproach, house style renders it as a comma: above reproach, not self-willed.
Elder in verse 5 and overseer here refer to the same office, viewed as mature standing and as watchful oversight.
The quotation Cretans are always liars is attributed to the Cretan poet-prophet Epimenides of Knossos, the so-called Epimenides paradox, as the BSB footnote notes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Titus 1:5-16. The em dash in verse 7 has been rendered as a comma per house style.
